title: The Languages of Africa
text:
This blog post explores the distribution of various languages spoken across the continent of Africa. The data was sourced from the tidytuesday Github repository, curated by Muwanga Robert: https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2026/2026-01-13/readme.md.
The data set consists of a variable for language which contains the name of an African language, a variable for family which contains a classification of that language according to ancestry, phonetics and grammar, a variable for native speakers which contains the total number of known native speakers of that language across the continent, and finally a variable for country which contains the name of the country where the language is spoken.
The question of interest for this exploration is: what is the language that has the most native speakers in Africa, and which region of Africa is it most spoken in?
To answer this question, a lollipop plot will be made to visualize the top 10 languages with the most native speakers, and the distribution of the one with the highest number of native speakers will be visualized on a map.



Bar plot to visualize the top 10 languages with the most native speakers









This bar plot shows Arabic to be the language with the most native speakers on the African continent by almost 3 times the number of native speakers of Hausa, which is the language that follows it in ranking by native speakers.
This is a surprising finding for me because I had expected Swahili to be one of the top 2 African languages with most native speakers. It is not so because although it is very widely spoken, a lot of its speakers speak it as a second or third language; they are not native speakers.
Here is a map to visualize the distribution of Arabic across the African continent.
Map showing the distribution of Arabic-speaking countries across the African continent


Reading layer `ne_10m_admin_0_countries' from data source 
  `C:\Users\msefa24\OneDrive - St. Lawrence University\SPRING 2026\DATA334\DataVis334\countries\ne_10m_admin_0_countries.shp' 
  using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
Simple feature collection with 258 features and 168 fields
Geometry type: MULTIPOLYGON
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: -180 ymin: -90 xmax: 180 ymax: 83.6341
Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84









From the map, it can be seen that countries that have native Arabic speakers are mostly in Northern Africa, except for Tanzania, Somalia and Djibouti which are in East Africa. This is likely the result of the 7th-8th century Arab-Islamic Expansion where Arab Muslim armies expanded out of the Arabian Peninsula into mostly Northern Africa and parts of East Africa.



The flaw in my approach to visualizing the distribution of countries that have native speakers of Arabic is that from first glance, and without prior understanding of the context of the visualization, a viewer might perceive the map as though it means the countries colored purple have Arabic as the only spoken language, whereas it is just one of the languages spoken in those countries. What this visualization was meant to do is to show which countries have native Arabic speakers.
If I had additional data on the number of native speakers for each language in every country it is spoken in, I would change the map to have a sequential color scheme to visualize the distribution of Arabic speakers across the continent according to their numbers.

This blog post explores the distribution of various languages spoken across the continent of Africa. The data was sourced from the tidytuesday Github repository, curated by Muwanga Robert: https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2026/2026-01-13/readme.md.

title: Trends in U.S. Gas Prices
text:
Introduction
This project explores a data set of fuel weekly fuel prices in the U.S. The data comes from the tidytuesday repository and the desciption said it was sourced from the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA). Here is the link for the tidytuesday repository from which this data was sourced: https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-07-01/readme.md
The data set contains a date variable which shows the day, month and year when the fuel prices were recorded, a fuel variable which is a categorical variable with the values gasoline and diesel, grade variable which is a categorical variable distinguishing the grade of gasoline or diesel, formulation which is a categorical variable describing the fuel’s formulation, and price which is a numerical variable showing the weekly price of fuel. The data set has 22, 360 observations.
In this data set, I am mostly interested in the date, fuel, grade and price variables to answer the following questions:

How have diesel and gasoline prices changed in the U.S. over time?
How have the prices of gasoline specifically differed by for different grades?

The grade variable has values all, regular, midgrade, or premium. All is a generalized representation of all the other grades (with price for such observations being the weighted average for all the other grades). To compare the grades more thoroughly without double counting, I will only visualize observations of grade “all”.

library(tidyverse)
library(broom)

weekly_gas_prices <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-07-01/weekly_gas_prices.csv')



Visualizations
To visualize the annual trends in average fuel prices for gasoline and diesel over the years, I filtered the data set to use only observations with grade == “all” because they are already encompassing the other grades.

prices_type <- weekly_gas_prices |> mutate(year = year(date))|>
  filter(grade == "all") |>
  group_by(year, fuel) |>
  summarise(avg_price = mean(price)) 

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by year and fuel.
ℹ Output is grouped by year.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(year, fuel))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

## visualization for trends in average fuel prices over the years

ggplot(data = prices_type, aes(x = year, y = avg_price)) +
  geom_line(aes(colour = fuel), linewidth = 1) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "Fuel prices over the years",
       x = "Year",
       y = "Average price")








Between 1994 and 2005, the average price of gasoline was consistently greater than the price of diesel. However, after 2005, the average price of diesel has been greater than the average price of gasoline.
There are three major dips in the trends of average prices of both diesel and gasoline around 2008, 2016 and 2020. My hypothesis is that these these align with the 2008 recession and the 2020 Covid pandemic. The dip in 2016 was caused by a surge in oil supply in the U.S. which led to price reductions.
From the line plot, it can be seen that the average prices of both diesel and gasoline have had an overall increase over the years despite the fluctuations.
To compare the trends in gasoline price according to grade:

grade_gas <- weekly_gas_prices |> mutate(year = year(date))|> 
  filter(fuel == "gasoline" & grade != "all") |>
  group_by(year, fuel, grade) |>
  summarise(average_price = mean(price)) 

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by year, fuel, and grade.
ℹ Output is grouped by year and fuel.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(year, fuel, grade))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

ggplot(data = grade_gas, aes(x = year, y = average_price, colour = grade)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "Trends in gasoline prices according to grade", 
       x = "Year", 
       y = "Average price")

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'









From the above scatter plot, it can be concluded that the price levels for gasoline differ according to grade over the years. The smoother for the prices of premium grade is the highest overall, followed by the prices for midgrade and lastly regular grade.


Conclusion:
Observations for midgrade and premium grade gasoline only start in 1994 whereas those of regular grade gasoline started from 1990. This does not show the full picture of the trends in prices by grade from 1990 to 1993, creating an incomplete analysis.
If I had more time, I would explore the trends in diesel prices over the years according to its grades just I did for gasoline.


Connection to Class Ideas
The visualizations I made use colors that are accessible for individuals with Color Vision Deficiency. I used a line plot to represent trends through time.

title: How Childcare Costs Vary Across the U.S.
text:
This is an exploration of a data set of childcare prices across the U.S. from 2008 to 2018. The data comes from the tidytuesday repository and the description said it was sourced from the National Database of Childcare Prices (NDCP). Here is the link for the tidytuesday repository from which this data was sourced: https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2023/2023-05-09/readme.md
The main data set consists of 34, 567 observations and 61 variables of childcare price data by childcare provider type, age of children, and county characteristics in the U.S. from 2008 to 2018. For the purpose of this exploration, this data set will be merged with a data set of U.S. counties and states according to their FIPS codes (unique numeric identifier for counties).
The data features two types of childcare providers: Center-based Care and Family Childcare. According to the Center for American Progress, Center-based Care is care offered in a nonresidential facilities like daycare centers where children are usually grouped into classrooms and supervised by multiple staff members, while Family Childcare is care provided in a private homes, often run by an individual provider.
In this data set, I am mostly interested in the study_year, county_name, state_name, and the variables for aggregated full-time median prices charged for Center-based Care and Family Childcare to study trends in childcare prices over time and across states, age groups and systems based on the following questions:

What is the spatial distribution of average childcare prices across U.S states?
What was the trend in prices of childcare between 2008 and 2018 in the state with the highest price levels?
How do childcare prices differ across age groups and childcare type?

It is worth noting that the prices provided in this data set are median weekly prices of childcare per county.

library(tidyverse)

childcare <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2023/2023-05-09/childcare_costs.csv')

USA <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2023/2023-05-09/counties.csv')

cost_df <- left_join(childcare, USA, by = join_by(county_fips_code))|>
  rename(mc_school_age = mcsa,
    mfcc_school_age = mfccsa)

cost_long <- cost_df |>  pivot_longer(cols = c(mc_school_age, mc_infant, mc_toddler, mc_preschool,
             mfcc_school_age, mfcc_infant, mfcc_toddler, mfcc_preschool),
    names_to = c("care_type", "age_group"),
    names_pattern = "(mc|mfcc)_(.*)",
    values_to = "price")

cost_long <- cost_long |> mutate(care_type = if_else(care_type == "mc", 
                                      true = "center-based", 
                                      false = "family care")) |>
  select(study_year, state_name, county_name, care_type, age_group, price)

title: How Childcare Costs Vary Across the U.S.
section: Differences by Age Group and Provider Type
text:
Differences by Age Group and Provider Type

#removing NA values for type comparisons
cost_long1 <- cost_long |> filter(!is.na(care_type), !is.na(age_group), !is.na(price)) |> 
  mutate(age_group = as.factor(age_group)) |>
  mutate(age_group = fct_relevel(age_group, "infant", "toddler", "preschool", "school_age"))


# comparing age group prices
ggplot(data = cost_long1, aes(x = age_group, y = price)) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.3, colour = "darkblue", fill = "skyblue") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Box Plot of weekly prices of childcare by age-group",
       x = "Age group",
       y = "weekly price")








Infant care tends to have higher median of prices and have outliers that tend to reach higher prices, while preschool and toddler care tend to have lower median prices, with preschool-age care having the least mean price of the four categories. This means that the cost of infant care tends more expensive than preschool and toddler care. Infants are 0 to 1 year olds, toddlers are children aged 1 to 3 years, preschoolers are aged 3 to 5 years, and school-aged children are 6 to 17 year olds. From the side-by-side boxplot above, it can be seen that the mean weekly price of childcare gets lower as the age group increases in years. This could be because the younger children require more intensive care than the older ones.

# comparing care types

ggplot(data = cost_long1, aes(x = care_type, y = price)) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.3, colour = "darkblue", fill = "skyblue") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Boxplot of weekly prices of childcare by Care Type",
       x = "Care Type",
       y = "weekly price")








From the boxplot above, it can be observed that the weekly prices for center-based childcare have a higher median and higher reaching outliers than family-based care. This suggests that center-based care tends to be more expensive than family-based child care. This could be because in center-based care, one provider cares for many children at once, therefore leading to more work which requires higher pay.

title: How Childcare Costs Vary Across the U.S.
text:
Geographic Variation in Childcare Costs
To assess the spatial distribution of median weekly childcare prices across the United States, across age ranges and childcare types, below is a choropleth map.

library(maps)
state_df <- ggplot2::map_data("state")

cost_st <- cost_long |> mutate(state_name = str_to_lower(state_name)) |>
  group_by(state_name) |>
  summarise(average_price = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE))

state_cost <- left_join(cost_st, state_df, by = c("state_name" = "region"))

ggplot(data = state_cost, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group)) +
  geom_polygon(colour = "black", aes(fill = average_price)) +
  coord_map(projection = "albers", lat0 = 39, lat1 = 45) +
  theme_void() +
  scale_fill_viridis_b() +
  labs(title = "Average childcare prices across the U.S.")








From the map above, it can be observed that the states of Indiana and New Mexico are the only two that have a grey color. This is because they did not have any childcare price data in this data set.
Massachusetts has the highest average weekly childcare prices of all states. Some of the states with the lowest average childcare prices are North Dakota and South Dakota.

title: How Childcare Costs Vary Across the U.S.
section: Childcare Price Trends Over Time in Massachusetts
text:
Childcare Price Trends Over Time in Massachusetts
To study the trends in childcare prices in Massachusetts between 2008 and 2018, the scatterplot below shows trends in weekly median prices of childcare in counties in Massachusetts over those years.

cost_mass <- cost_long |> filter(state_name == "Massachusetts") |>
   mutate(age_group = fct_relevel(age_group, "infant", "toddler", 
                                  "preschool", "school_age"))

ggplot(data = cost_mass, aes(x = study_year, y = price)) +
 geom_point(aes(colour = age_group), alpha = 0.4, size = 2) +         
  geom_smooth(aes(colour = age_group), se = FALSE, size = 1) +        
  facet_wrap(~care_type) +      
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2008, 2018, 1)) + 
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
   theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Weekly Childcare Prices in Massachusetts Counties (2008–2018)",
    caption = "Trends shown by Age Group and Care Type",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Weekly Price")

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.


`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'









The plot above shows childcare prices to have generally increased between 2008 and 2018 for each age-group. However, the childcare prices for school-age children remains the lowest of all 4 categories, with the highest being infant care.
Weekly median prices of center-based childcare are generally higher than family-based care for infant, toddler and preschool aged children in Massachusetts throughout the years. However, childcare for school-aged children used to be much lower in center-based systems than family-care between 2008 and 2011, then it increased rapidly to where it slightly surpassed family-based care prices by 2018.

title: How Childcare Costs Vary Across the U.S.
text:
Conclusion
From this exploration of childcare prices across the U.S. and on a smaller scale, in Massachusetts, it can be concluded that weekly median childcare prices differ according to different age-groups of children and the two different childcare systems. Center-based childcare appeared to be typically more expensive than family-based childcare, which might be due to the cost of paying for facilities in a center and staff salaries since centers like day-care centers usually hire multiple workers. Infant care also appeared to consistently be more expensive than all 3 other age-group categories. This might be because infants need more continuous and hands-on care than the older children.
Potential improvements to this project could be creating a linear regression model to visualize the relationship between price of childcare and some of the variables such as age_group and others.

title: How Childcare Costs Vary Across the U.S.
text:
Connection to Class Concepts
I used a choropleth map to visualize spatial distribution of average weekly childcare prices throughout the U.S. The visualizations I made use colors that are accessible for individuals with Color Vision Deficiency.
The limitation with this data set is that the raw data provided median weekly prices not actual prices, so the explorations above are based on weekly median childcare prices, therefore, we do not have access to the original sample size and variability.

The data for the year 2025 is incomplete for all countries.The data set containing monthly data only consists of 4 to 6 months’ data for 2025 for all countries, implying that at the time this data set was published, the data collection process for 2025 was not yet completed. So, I have filtered out the year 2025 to eliminate these incomplete records for the sake of consistency throughout the years.
To explore the questions of interest mentioned above, the following sections use visualizations such as line plots and choropleth maps to display trends in measles cases over time and across the world.

title: The Global Case Of Measles
section: The global trend in total cases of measles over the 13 years from 2012 to 2024
text:
The global trend in total cases of measles over the 13 years from 2012 to 2024









The line plot above shows a fluctuating trend in total measles cases reported across the world between 2012 and 2024. There are three major peaks around 2014, 2019 and 2024. These suggest that there were some outbreaks or surges in total reported cases in the world in those periods. There are two major dips int he trend between these three peaks, corresponding to 2016-2017 and 2020-2021. Interestingly, in 2020-2021, measles cases reported were the lowest in that 13-year period. Since 2020-2021 is the time notoriously known for the Covid-19 pandemic when people were “forced” to isolate, this may have decreased the rate of infections of measles as people did not come into contact with infected individuals. However, there is also a possibility that the low records of measles cases in that period just reflect the fact that medical resources and reporting was aimed more towards tracking the Covid-19 pandemic and measles reporting was not prioritized as much as in other years.
As of 2024, the total cases of measles across the world were on a sharp rise. This contradicts human expectations that as the world at large advances technologically and medically, the spread of preventable diseases like measles would decrease.
This increasing trend in measles cases begs the question: which countries contribute the most to this growth? To answer this, below is a choropleth map that shows the global distribution of total measles cases.






Looking at the map above, most of the world’s countries had less than 10,000 reported cases in 2024. However a select few reached 20,000 cases and above. As you hover over the map, you can see the names of each country and the total number of cases it reported in 2024.
The barplot below shows the top 10 countries with the highest total number of measles cases reported in 2024 for easier comparison.






# A tibble: 1 × 10
  region country iso3   year total_population measles_total
  <chr>  <chr>   <chr> <dbl>            <dbl>         <dbl>
1 AFRO   Namibia NAM    2024          3030131            74
# ℹ 4 more variables: measles_lab_confirmed <dbl>, measles_epi_linked <dbl>,
#   measles_clinical <dbl>,
#   measles_incidence_rate_per_1000000_total_population <dbl>


From the plot above, we can observe that in 2024, Iraq reported the highest number of cases in the world with 32400 cases.
The World Health Organization (WHO) groups its member states into these 6 regions: Africa (AFRO), the Americas (AMRO), South-East Asia (SEARO), Europe (EURO), Eastern Mediterranean (EMRO), and the Western Pacific (WPRO). According to WHO, these regions do not strictly follow continental boundaries. As seen on the plot above, Kyrgyzstan, which is in Central Asia is categorized under the EURO region.
From the barplot, half of the top ten countries with the highest reported cases of measles in 2024 were in the EURO region, 3 were in the EMRO region and there was only 1 country for the SEARO and AFRO regions each.
Although total reported cases provide an indication of the spread of the disease throughout a country, they are not necessarily a good metric for comparing the burden of the disease among various countries with different populations. A total count of 32400 in Iraq in 2024 where the population was around 46 million and a total count of 74 in Namibia where the population was around 3 million in 2024 do not give a fair comparison of the prevalence of the disease and level of risk for the people in that country.
So, to assess this risk and prevalence, the plots below use incidence rate per 1 million of the population to compare measles reports between countries for the year 2024.
Incidence rate is a measure of new cases of a disease within a particular population over a set period of time. In this case, incidence rate measures the number of new case per 1 million of aeach country’s population for a given year.












From the choropleth map and the barplot of the top ten countries with the highest incidence rate above, we can see that incidence rates per million of the population tell a different story from that told by total reported cases. Kyrgyzstan is now at the top with the highest incidence rate (2005 cases per million). This means that in 2024, people in Kyrgyzstan were at the highest risk of being infected with measles as compared to other countries.
The EURO region still has the highest number of countries even in the top ten countries with the highest incidence rates.
The bar chart confirms the patterns observed in the choropleth map, with countries such as Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Azerbaijan, and Romania exhibiting the highest incidence rates. This observation led me to discover that there was in fact a measles outbreak in the EURO region (European and Central Asian Region) in 2024 as reported on a press release by UNICEF.
